metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC1 to state the concrete observable that proves 'mirrors': e.g., 'the op-cert check runs inside the same job step / loop body as the identity check in verify-identity.yaml, confirmed by a grep/diff showing a single merged-log iteration covering both checks'.
findings.finding: AC1 uses 'mirrors the shipped gate' as its success description, but 'mirrors' is outcome-vague — it could mean same file, same loop, same env-var name, or same logic. An executor has no concrete observable to check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1
findings.suggested_fix: AC1: 'The op-cert required-environment check is implemented as an additional check block inside the existing merged-log `for` loop in `.github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml` (no new parallel job), verified by `grep -c "ENFORCE_SINCE" .github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml` returning 1 (one shared grandfathering gate).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC2: (a) define 'foreign cert' (e.g., a cert signed for a different environment/project), (b) name the fixture file(s) and the command to execute them (e.g., `pytest tests/test_verify_identity.py::test_op_cert_missing test_op_cert_foreign`).
findings.finding: AC2 names a 'fixture test' as its proving mechanism but does not identify the fixture file, its location, what inputs it covers (missing-cert case and foreign-cert case), or the command to run it. 'Foreign cert' is also undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For AC3, specify the output artifact and its location (e.g., a table in the PR description listing each trigger and its INCLUDED/NO_FILTER verdict). For AC4, define the classification categories (e.g., ENFORCED/GRANDFATHERED/OUT-OF-SCOPE) and name the output artifact.
findings.finding: AC3 (CI-trigger audit) and AC4 (merge call-site enumeration) specify no output artifact, no location, and no proving command, leaving them verifiable only by subjective inspection.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3 and AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete implementation artifact (module path, schema, or CLI subcommand) for the required-environment certificate verification that the op-cert lane will build on or create, so AC2's fixture test has a grounded target.
findings.finding: The plan's AC2 and Context refer to verifying the 'required-environment certificate', but no corresponding implementation symbol, schema field, or CLI sub-command for op-cert / required-environment verification exists anywhere in the codebase (no `required_environment`, `verify_op_cert`, or op-cert command handler was found). The plan provides no pointer to what code artifact the new CLI lane would be built on or extend, leaving the edit target for the actual implementation underspecified.
findings.location: AC2 — 'gate verifies the required-environment certificate over the merged log'; Context — 'op-cert verification lane'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the required-environment certificate's structure (storage field name, payload type, DSSE envelope format or alternative) and add it to the plan or link to the op-cert epic's schema definition.
findings.finding: The plan names a 'required-environment certificate' as the object being verified, but no schema, field name, or structural definition for this certificate type exists in the codebase or is specified in the plan. The identity lane's equivalent — the DSSE authorship envelope stored as 'author_sig' on an event — is fully specified (authorship.py, dsse.py, verify_identity_report.schema.json). The op-cert analog has no defined storage field, payload type, or DSSE envelope structure.
findings.location: Context and AC2: 'required-environment certificate'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific `rebar` CLI subcommand (e.g. `rebar verify-op-cert`) that will implement the op-cert lane, confirm it will be added to the CLI intercept table and the `attest/registry.py` POLICY dict, and gate the full plan on that surface existing (or add a SPIKE task).
findings.finding: The plan asserts an 'op-cert verification lane' will be added to verify-identity.yaml but never names the specific rebar CLI subcommand/flag that implements the check. No `verify-op-cert`, `rebar cert`, or op-cert-specific command exists in the CLI today (the CLI reference and all intercepts were exhaustively checked). The `attest/registry.py` POLICY dict has no op-cert kind registered either. The plan commits to a full implementation without identifying this gap as a SPIKE.
findings.location: AC1 / Context ('Add an op-cert verification lane to that SAME gate')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a prerequisite task: 'Implement `rebar verify-op-cert --require-certified --since <ref>` as a new CLI intercept (mirroring verify-identity's shape) and register an `op-cert` kind + policy in `attest/registry.py`.' Then reference that command in the verify-identity.yaml `run:` step and in the AC2 fixture test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the auth/trust-root precondition for the op-cert lane: identify where allowed op-cert signers are stored (e.g., a repo variable, an `allowed_signers`-format file, or an in-band ticket field) and how that trust root is passed to the verifier in CI.
findings.finding: The plan does not state auth/key-material preconditions for the op-cert lane. The required-environment certificate verification needs a trust root (which keys/principals are trusted to certify an environment). The existing identity gate derives its trust root from the author identity ticket's `keys` field (in-band). The op-cert lane's equivalent trust-root source is unspecified — the plan never says where the allowed op-cert signers come from or what environment variable/repo secret makes them available in CI.
findings.location: AC2 / Context ('verifies the required-environment certificate over the merged log')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Context assertion: verify whether the shipped `verify-identity.yaml` does a full-store scan (no `--base`) or a `<base>..HEAD` walk, and update the 'one merged-log walk' claim and AC-1 to match the actual behavior before designing the op-cert lane's scoping.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the shipped gate 'walks the merged log', but the actual workflow command (`rebar verify-identity --require-authenticated --since "${ENFORCE_SINCE}"`) passes no `--base` argument; the CLI code shows that without `--base`, it calls `_collect_all(tracker)` — a full store scan, not a `<base>..HEAD` merged-log walk. The architectural premise that op-cert can share 'one merged-log walk' with the identity lane rests on this mischaracterization.
findings.location: Context section: 'which walks the merged log and grandfathers via ENFORCE_SINCE'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Context to read: 'which scans the full ticket store (no --base) and grandfathers via ENFORCE_SINCE'. Then decide explicitly whether the op-cert lane should also use full-store scan or a --base commit-range scan, and reflect that in AC-1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document what concrete changes to `verify_authorship.py` (or a new sibling command) are needed to add an op-cert verification lane — the plan currently asserts reuse as trivial but no extension point exists in the shipped code.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Add an op-cert verification lane to that SAME gate' as if an extension point exists in the shipped gate, but no op-cert verification command, no `OpCertConfig` dataclass, no `op_cert.enforce_since` config field, and no second-lane dispatch mechanism exist anywhere in the codebase. The architectural reuse claim is entirely unverified.
findings.location: Context section: 'Add an op-cert verification lane to that SAME gate'; AC-1: 'The op-cert required-environment check is added to / mirrors the shipped verify-identity.yaml gate'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether op-cert grandfathering shares `ENFORCE_SINCE`/`identity.enforce_since` or requires a distinct config key (e.g. `op_cert.enforce_since` / `OP_CERT_ENFORCE_SINCE`); add the needed config/workflow variable to the plan if they must be independent.
findings.finding: The plan asserts '`ENFORCE_SINCE`-style grandfathering covers both kinds', implying a single `ENFORCE_SINCE` variable governs both identity and op-cert. But `ENFORCE_SINCE` maps to `identity.enforce_since` in `IdentityConfig` only; no op-cert grandfathering config field exists. If the two lanes need independent cutover dates (likely during staged rollout), the plan provides no mechanism for it.
findings.location: Context: 'one merged-log walk + grandfathering'; AC-1: 'ENFORCE_SINCE-style grandfathering covers both kinds'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and enumerate the op-cert lane's verdict/classification vocabulary (e.g. `certified`, `missing_cert`, `foreign_cert`, `malformed_cert`) analogous to the identity lane's constants, and register the op-cert kind in `attest/registry.POLICY` with a pinned scheme and namespace.
findings.finding: AC2 references 'missing/foreign cert' as gate-failure triggers for the new op-cert lane, but these are not defined as an enumerated verdict/classification vocabulary anywhere — unlike the identity lane's fully-enumerated constants (VERIFIED, UNSIGNED, BAD_SIGNATURE, KEY_NOT_VALID_AT_ERA, UNKNOWN_AUTHOR in verify_authorship.py). Tool search finds no op-cert kind string, no POLICY entry for op-cert in attest/registry.py, and no CLI subcommand for op-cert verification. An executor implementing this lane has no defined verdict enum to emit.
findings.location: AC2: 'fails on a missing/foreign cert (fixture test)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to the plan (or a referenced spec file) that: (1) names the op-cert attestation kind string (e.g. `rebar.op-cert.v1`); (2) enumerates all possible verdict strings the lane can emit; (3) defines 'foreign cert' precisely (e.g. cert present but signed by a key not in the environment's trust root); (4) adds a corresponding POLICY entry in attest/registry.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that exercises the gate end-to-end in CI (e.g., a test PR or `act`-based replay against the workflow YAML with a synthetic missing-cert commit) to confirm the gate actually fails at the merge step, not only in an offline fixture.
findings.finding: AC2's proving mechanism is a fixture test, which may run offline/mocked and therefore not exercise the live CI gate path. The plan has no criterion that exercises the gate end-to-end in an actual (or simulated) merge scenario.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2; Context section
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'A synthetic test branch lacking an op-cert is run through the gate (via `act -j verify-identity` or a draft PR in the repo) and the job exits non-zero, producing a log line matching `ERROR.*op-cert.*missing`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full lifecycle for the op-cert `ENFORCE_SINCE`-style grandfathering config: who creates it (and with what initial value), whether it is a shared or separate variable from the identity lane's value, who owns updates, and when/how it is retired once all pre-cutoff commits are out of scope.
findings.finding: The plan introduces an `ENFORCE_SINCE`-style grandfathering value for the op-cert lane but does not specify who creates it, what its initial value is, whether it is shared with or distinct from the identity epic's existing `ENFORCE_SINCE`, who has update ownership, or whether it accumulates/leaks over time.
findings.location: AC line 1 / Context section: 'one merged-log walk + ENFORCE_SINCE-style grandfathering covers both kinds'
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a distinct `OP_CERT_ENFORCE_SINCE` env var (separate from the identity lane's variable) set in the workflow file at the time the feature lands. Document in the plan: (1) initial value = merge date of this PR, (2) ownership = security team rotates it, (3) retirement = remove the variable and the guard clause once 100% of active branches post-date the cutoff (tracked as a follow-up ticket).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the op-cert lane reuses the existing `ENFORCE_SINCE`/`identity.enforce_since` grandfathering boundary or introduces a distinct op-cert-scoped key; name the key explicitly and justify whether shared or split grandfathering is intentional.
findings.finding: The plan says 'one merged-log walk + ENFORCE_SINCE-style grandfathering covers both kinds' but does not specify whether the op-cert lane shares the existing ENFORCE_SINCE repo variable / identity.enforce_since config key with the identity lane, or introduces a parallel op-cert-scoped key. These two options have incompatible trade-offs: a shared key conflates independent enforcement timelines (an operator cannot grandfather identity separately from op-cert), while a new key proliferates config surface. The existing config schema has identity.enforce_since typed under IdentityConfig — a second lane's boundary belongs in a distinct namespace if they can diverge.
findings.location: AC line 1 / Context: 'one merged-log walk + ENFORCE_SINCE-style grandfathering covers both kinds'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether op-cert grandfathering uses its own variable (e.g. `OP_CERT_ENFORCE_SINCE`) independent of the authorship `ENFORCE_SINCE`, or justify with a rationale why a shared boundary is correct for all deployments.
findings.finding: The plan states 'one merged-log walk + ENFORCE_SINCE-style grandfathering covers both kinds', implicitly sharing the single ENFORCE_SINCE repo variable between the op-cert and authorship lanes. These are independent enforcement timelines: a project may wish to grandfather op-cert adoption without also grandfathering identity enforcement (or vice versa). Sharing one boundary variable couples them — projects cannot enable op-cert enforcement without also freezing the authorship grandfather, a hidden ordering assumption that can force premature enforcement of one lane.
findings.location: AC1: 'one merged-log walk + ENFORCE_SINCE-style grandfathering covers both kinds'; Context: 'Add an op-cert verification lane to that SAME gate (one merged-log walk + grandfathering)'
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a separate `OP_CERT_ENFORCE_SINCE` repo variable fed to the op-cert lane's `--since`-equivalent flag, mirroring the pattern already established by the identity lane, so the two enforcement timelines remain independently configurable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a description in the plan body for (a) how to conduct the CI-trigger audit (which triggers, what INCLUDED/NO_FILTER means, where results are recorded) and (b) how to enumerate and classify merge call-sites (scope, classification rubric, output artifact).
findings.finding: AC3 ('CI-trigger audit') and AC4 ('enumerates and classifies existing merge call-sites') describe deliverables — a trigger classification exercise and a call-site enumeration — that have no corresponding description in the plan body (Context). The Context only describes the gate mechanics; it says nothing about how trigger classification or call-site enumeration will be performed, who owns them, or what their outputs look like.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 and AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In the Context or a new 'Approach' section, add one sentence per deliverable: e.g., 'Trigger audit: scan .github/workflows/ for every `on:` event referencing the gate and mark each INCLUDED (fires on merge) or NO_FILTER (unrestricted); record in a table in the PR description.' and 'Call-site enumeration: grep the repo for merge API calls / GitHub Actions steps that invoke merge and classify each as (a) already gated, (b) newly gated, or (c) out-of-scope.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'foreign cert' in the plan body (e.g., a cert whose issuing environment does not match the required environment for the ticket) and describe the detection logic and fixture that exercises this failure path.
findings.finding: AC2 requires the gate to 'fail on a missing/foreign cert' and to be verified via a fixture test, but the plan body only describes failure on a missing cert ('fails when a ticket that must carry one does not'). The 'foreign cert' case (a cert issued for a different environment) is an additional scenario with no description of what constitutes a 'foreign' cert, how it is detected, or what fixture exercises it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 vs. Context paragraph 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Context or a new 'Verification' subsection: 'A "foreign cert" is one whose environment field does not match the project's declared required-environment. Detection: compare cert.environment against the project config at verification time. Fixture: a test ticket carrying a cert stamped for environment B in a project requiring environment A must cause gate failure.'

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out mechanism for the new merge-gate enforcement (e.g., a repository-level env variable kill switch, an admin bypass label, or a documented emergency disable procedure) so the gate can be safely disabled if misconfigured.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new enforcement prohibition ('newly forbids landing an un-certified change in high-security projects') — a breaking behavioral change — but provides no rollback or back-out plan beyond the ENFORCE_SINCE grandfathering. Grandfathering covers historical commits, not a scenario where the gate is misconfigured or produces false positives and must be disabled quickly without reverting all gate-related commits.
findings.location: Context — 'This newly forbids landing an un-certified change in high-security projects'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Context: 'Back-out: the gate respects a repository variable OP_CERT_GATE_DISABLED=1 (settable by repo admins) that causes it to emit a warning and exit 0, allowing merges to proceed while a fix is deployed. This variable is audited and requires two-admin approval to set.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback/fail-closed specification for the op-cert lane: define the gate behavior (verdict + exit code) when cert verification itself fails due to infrastructure errors (unavailable tool, unrecognized cert format, unresolvable trust root).
findings.finding: The plan specifies no fallback behavior for the new op-cert verification lane when the sub-step fails (e.g., the op-cert CLI tool/function is unavailable, the cert format is unrecognized, or the trust root cannot be resolved). The identity lane has explicit fail-closed fallbacks at every sub-step (resolve_trust_root returns None on any error, verify_authorship_at_commit catches all exceptions, verify_event_authorship returns a non-verified Verdict on parse failure). The plan's AC2 only names 'missing/foreign cert' as failure triggers, leaving infrastructure-level failures unspecified.
findings.location: AC2: 'fails on a missing/foreign cert (fixture test)'
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the identity lane's pattern: specify that any exception/lookup-failure in the op-cert lane emits a non-verified verdict (e.g., `cert_error`) and causes gate failure under enforcement, rather than silently passing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md/design-doc entry capturing the decision to co-locate the op-cert lane in `verify-identity.yaml` (rationale, ENFORCE_SINCE grandfathering contract, cert interface format, and what constitutes a 'high-security project').
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — adding an op-cert verification lane to the merge gate — but does not call for capturing this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc, leaving the rationale (why same gate, why ENFORCE_SINCE grandfathering, what the cert contract is) undocumented for future maintainers.
findings.location: Context section / AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC item: 'An ADR (or equivalent design-doc section) is created documenting the op-cert gate architecture: co-location in verify-identity.yaml, the ENFORCE_SINCE grandfathering contract, the required-environment cert schema, and the high-security project scope definition.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where the high-security project scope and required-environment cert validation rules are defined (e.g., a config file, repository topic, or environment variable) so policy updates don't require workflow code changes.
findings.finding: The rules governing which projects are 'high-security' (subject to the gate) and what constitutes a valid required-environment certificate are not specified as configurable — these are business rules expected to evolve as the cert scheme matures, and hardcoding them would require code changes for each policy update.
findings.location: AC2 / AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either enumerate the existing merge call-sites (paths, scripts, or external integrations) that AC4 promises to classify, or clarify what 'merge call-sites' refers to (e.g., Gerrit webhook, GitHub Actions `push` trigger, direct CLI invocations) so the prohibition scope is grounded.
findings.finding: AC4 states 'the new prohibition enumerates and classifies existing merge call-sites', but the plan itself contains no such enumeration, and a codebase search finds no `rebar merge` command or `merge` call-site in the CLI router or `_commands/` directory. The call-sites that AC4 promises to enumerate are neither listed in the plan nor groundable in the current code.
findings.location: AC4 — 'enumerates and classifies existing merge call-sites'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the combined-lane approach (e.g., 'one log parse avoids duplicating the O(n) walk cost; shared trigger configuration reduces maintenance surface; aligns with ADR 0044's one-substrate design').
findings.finding: The plan's rationale for the combined-lane approach is expressed only negatively ('rather than standing up a parallel walker'), with no positive rationale articulating why this is the better choice (e.g., performance, trigger-configuration simplicity, shared walker infrastructure, or blast-radius). The rubric requires a positive rationale; its absence means an implementer cannot verify the approach's suitability or defend it in review.
findings.location: Context section: 'Add an op-cert verification lane to that SAME gate (one merged-log walk + grandfathering) rather than standing up a parallel walker.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture of the ENFORCE_SINCE value and any cert-verification config: state explicitly that these are stored outside PR-author write reach (e.g., as repository-level environment secrets/variables, or in a branch-protected base file that GitHub Actions workflow permissions prevent PR branches from overriding) so a PR author cannot self-grandfather an un-certified commit.
findings.finding: The plan does not declare the access posture of the ENFORCE_SINCE grandfathering configuration — specifically, it does not state whether the grandfathering boundary and cert-verification config are write-protected against PR authors. External contributors who can open PRs represent a lower-trust actor that can trigger this gate; if any of the gate's configuration inputs (e.g., ENFORCE_SINCE value, allowlist) live in files that a PR branch can modify, a contributor could craft a PR that self-grandfathers an un-certified commit and causes the gate to incorrectly pass.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('ENFORCE_SINCE-style grandfathering') and Context section ('grandfathers via ENFORCE_SINCE')
findings.suggested_fix: Store ENFORCE_SINCE and cert-verification config as GitHub repository-level environment variables or protected branch configs (not as committed files on the PR branch). Document in the plan: 'ENFORCE_SINCE is a repo-level env var settable only by maintainers; PR branches cannot override it.' Add a note to AC1 that the grandfathering boundary is write-protected from PR authors.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
